title: First Worlds
html:
The Shop has been open in front of me long enough that I'm still on page one.

Not because the entries are confusing. Each item lists its name, cost, and what it requires, all clearly laid out. The problem is the list gives no indication of what to do first, and getting the order wrong when I have exactly 1,000 points and an unspecified loan to repay at some unknown point seems like the kind of mistake I can't walk back.

I scroll to the top and start again.

The cheapest things in the Shop are what you'd expect from cheapest. Ground cover at 10 Life Energy: suitable for bare soil or rock, generates a small amount of Life Energy per cycle, requires a surface and a stable atmosphere. Basic grass at 15: same conditions, slightly better output. Simple shrubs at 30, and from there prices climb at a rate that makes 1,000 feel finite. At the very bottom of the page, in smaller text than the item listings, there's a note I missed the first time through.

---

[Note: Life Energy generation rates shown are per cycle. Cycle length is determined by placement environment conditions. A stable environment shortens cycle length. An unstable environment extends it. Conditions can be viewed through the Life function.]

---

I close the Shop and open Life.

Previously I'd seen two numbers: current Life Energy, total life count in the constellation. There's a third option I hadn't clicked, a small icon sitting next to the constellation name. I click it now.

The void doesn't shake or flash. It just shifts, and suddenly I'm looking at something that isn't nothing. My constellation, rendered at a scale I can take in. A dim central star sitting at the middle of it, giving off the kind of light that suggests it has been at this for a very long time. Three planets at varying distances around it, none doing anything in particular. The nearest is roughly the size of the ones I've seen in school textbooks, grey and dry, rotating with no hurry. The second is smaller and further out, a faint band of debris in its orbit. The third sits even further back, partially obscured.

I select the nearest one.

The view closes in and I'm looking at the surface. Grey soil in every direction, flat, the sky the same grey-white above it. No features I can pick out. The atmosphere panel beside the view reads: breathable, barely. Temperature: wide range, cold enough at night to matter. Gravity: slightly below what I'm used to.

The conditions panel sits beneath that. Soil quality: minimum. Moisture: dry. Stability rating: a progress bar sitting in the lower third, not alarming but not good. The note in the Shop said cycle length tracks with stability. This planet won't run fast cycles in its current state.

'If I just drop grass on it now the return will be terrible.'

I go back to the Shop and this time look under a tab I hadn't opened: Terrain. Most of it is priced for a later version of me with substantially more resources. Near the top of the list, though: basic soil improvement, 50 Life Energy. It raises soil quality by one tier over a duration, which in turn improves plant growth rate and Life Energy output.

50 is a real chunk of 1,000. But it makes everything placed after it work better, which means the math on waiting is worse than the math on spending now.

I buy the soil improvement and apply it to Planet 1.

---

[Terrain modification initiated. Basic soil improvement applied to Planet 1. Duration: 3 cycles. Soil quality will improve by 1 tier upon completion.]

---

950 Life Energy remaining. I open the planet surface view again and look at the grey soil. Nothing visibly different yet. The soil improvement is running under the surface at whatever speed 3 cycles measures out to. I haven't checked how long a cycle runs in real time. I file that under things to find out.

Plants next. The logic is straightforward to anyone who's read enough fiction about starting worlds from scratch, which I have. You put down plant life before anything that eats or uses plant life. Ground cover first because it costs least and asks least from the soil. Grass after because it generates slightly more and spreads into whatever ground cover establishes. I buy both and place them across the flat main zone of the planet, grass clustered toward the warmer equatorial band where the temperature panel shows the highest daily average.

---

[Basic ground cover purchased. Basic grass purchased. Items placed on Planet 1.]

---

925 Life Energy. The Life counter in the bottom of the screen reads 0.0000, which technically means something is there now, just too small to round up to anything legible. I look at the surface view. The planet looks exactly the same.

There is still no water. The atmosphere panel marks the planet as dry and the conditions panel has moisture at the bottom of its range. Dry soil generates less from plant life and plant life doesn't spread well in it. I go back to the Shop and search for water. Three results come back: basic water deposit at 120 Life Energy, river system at 400, ocean basin at 850. Two of those three are academic at my current budget.

120 for the water deposit puts me at 805. With 1,000 in, spending 195 in the first session is either confident or reckless and the difference mostly depends on whether the plants grow as the panel says they will.

I buy it and place it in the warmest zone, about a third of the way across the flat. The surface view updates and a dark circle appears in the grey, small, still, sitting there like a puddle that hasn't been told it's not big enough to matter.

---

[Basic water deposit placed. Adjacent soil moisture increasing. Plant life growth rate adjusted upward.]

---

805 Life Energy. I note the number and open Help to check cycle duration. It takes three pages of scrolling to find the relevant entry: cycle length is environment-dependent, and at current conditions Planet 1 runs approximately 72 hours per cycle.

So the soil improvement finishes in roughly 9 days. The ground cover and grass are already generating, slowly, and will generate faster once the soil tier improves. The water deposit is raising moisture in the strip around it, which the conditions panel confirms is already adjusting.

I sit there, on whatever is passing for a floor in this void, doing the arithmetic. 805 remaining. Two untouched planets. A soil cycle running a 9-day timer. No information on when the loan comes due or how the repayment is structured. The system has not mentioned repayment once since the loan was granted, which is either reassuring or a reason to be significantly less comfortable.

I open Help and search for loan. There are four entries about the loan system. The first three cover the mechanics of accessing and checking the loan balance, plus a note that the loan is interest-free for the first cycle of the loan itself, not the planetary cycle. The fourth entry is the one I want: repayment terms.

---

[Loan repayment begins after 100 cycles from the date of activation. The minimum repayment per cycle thereafter is 10% of outstanding balance. Failure to meet minimum repayment will result in a reduction of available Shop functions.]

---

100 cycles. At 72 hours per cycle that works out to roughly 300 days. About 10 months. 10% of whatever I've borrowed per cycle after that, until it's cleared. The interest-free window is long and the repayment rate is slow enough to manage if the generation is working by then.

'Better than a credit card.'

I close Help and check the surface of Planet 1 again. The dark circle of the water deposit sits in the grey, and at its edge there's a narrow strip where the color of the soil is shifting, just at the point where moisture reaches furthest. Lighter. Not dramatically, not enough that a photo would show a difference, but different from how it looked 10 minutes ago.

The Life counter ticks forward. Four decimal places, a number so small it barely registers, but the last digit goes up by one.

I open the Log.

---

[A void of nothingness.]

[Constellation manager appointed.]

[Terrain modification initiated on Planet 1.]

[Basic ground cover placed on Planet 1.]

[Basic grass placed on Planet 1.]

[Basic water deposit placed on Planet 1.]

[Life Energy remaining: 805.]

---

I read through it once and close it.

805 Life Energy, one planet seeded and running. Two others untouched with their own unknown conditions. The soil improvement cycle has started and won't finish for 9 days. The moisture strip at the edge of the water deposit is still expanding outward, doing what it's supposed to do without being asked about it.

I zoom the constellation view out until I can see all three planets at once. The nearest one: mine now, in whatever sense a rock with a patch of damp soil can belong to someone. The other two sit at their distances, unlit by anything except the dim star, waiting.

I don't know what their conditions look like. Either one could be worse than Planet 1. Either could be better.

I select Planet 2 and the view closes in.
